author: ZiggyMUD
content:
.
If Z.ink remains a $300-400M FDV blockchain with a good 4X game running on it, Z.ink would be a top gaming chain with real player volume (it's not a gaming chain but it will likely be put on CMC list anyways)
Would this not catch some attention?
Would a higher FDV not help to drive other games to join Z.ink?
Would a higher FDV not change sentiment about Star Atlas?

What's 5% of $300M? = $15M for the POLIS DAO + validator revenue
If POLIS is only valued at $6.65M FDV but it holds $15-20M in Z.ink in its treasury, what should the price of POLIS do?
If the price of POLIS trends higher to reprice, does the chart look better, will more people want to buy POLIS to lock and earn zXP for the second, third and fourth airdrop?

If buying a ship from ATMTA give you X number of zXP could that zXP equal more than the price of the ship?
If buying ATLAS and locking it give you more in zXP than the cost of ATLAS will you buy more ATLAS?

If all of this is kinda true and C4 is fun and runs well should the player base increase at least back to 1500 like we had?

If Polis and Atlas bottom and start trending up and we don't sell, ATMTA stops selling ATLAS because they no longer have any, where does the value of the current player base assets go? Where does sentiment then go?

If the top 100 players can decide together that they want Z.ink and ATMTA and Star Atlas to succeed then not selling our Z.ink could be the spark we have all benn hoping for!

author: [SAI] Urto666
content: Sorry for the length   Yeah this is why Z.ink having a good launch and high FDV is so important. Let's say after the Z.ink TGE you can check what your airdrop is worth and it kinda blows your mind, its a lot higher than you were expecting (at least for most or outsiders), then players will not be buying or playing to earn ATLAS primarily, they will be doing it for zXP. Let's take you as an example: If Z.ink launches with a $300M FDV and the airdrop is worth $30M your share is likely around 0.009% or $270,000   Then you have to consider that some players above you will be getting a airdrop of over $1-5M Will players be willing to put more money in then?  Also consider this is at a $300M FDV not a $400FDV Consider that no VCs will have any tokens, no early investors that are just looking for the money, no CEXs like binance, just the DAO, Z.ink & ATMTA teams and the most diehard community members Ok so what does it matter... Everything. It means that the community members that receive these airdrops will determine how well Z.ink performs, for each of the following airdrops.  Ok so you get $270K from the first airdrop. So if you and everyone else holds, the second airdrop could be worth equal or more. Why? Assuming the following airdrops are 10% each and there are 4 in total, then by everyone holding their airdrop we control where the price goes (especially the top 100 players) and if we think both the headlines of the large amounts everyone receives from the airdrop, plus a game that is getting much better attacks other players, plus lower fees on Z.ink can drive some attention to Star Atlas then there should be some demand for Z.ink and prices should remain at least relatively level. So the second airdrop could end up being similar in size as the first one, and the third and fourth. Yes the following airdrops could be smaller than 10%, but im trying to explain that the top 100 players will control the price of Z.ink. I understand everyone wants to earn money.

author: ZiggyMUD
content:
.

Some caveats:A portion of "MM volume" is legitimate (providing real depth), but crypto has history of inflated/fake volume via wash trading (bots trading back-and-forth with themselves) — estimates for fake volume on some exchanges historically hit 70–95%, though it's declined on top regulated platforms.
Wash trading is now lower on major CEXs/DEXs (Chainalysis/TRM reports show illicit/fake activity as ~1–2% of total in recent years), but it still happens on smaller venues or perp DEXs chasing points/farming.
Institutional flow (big funds, whales) now drives ~60%+ of overall crypto volume in many reports, blending with MM activity.

In short: Market makers are the "plumbing" keeping markets running smoothly — they generate a large majority of volume on most tokens (especially non-bluechips), while "real" traders (retail + institutions taking directional bets) provide the spark that moves prices meaningfully. Without MMs, trading would be slow, expensive, and volatile; without real traders, volume would be fake and meaningless.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content:
Can confirm maximum wreckage.  I've swapped to the custom RPC which mostly resolved the read layer but sending TX is very bad. Block height errors are like 90% of my tx.  I haven't seen these errors for like 2+ years back when Solana was congested.

So far my general work arounds are:

1) anyone with large amounts of ruchs/airbikes need to disband them immediately as they take up most of the rpc bandwidth when loading fleets

2) using custom rpc Wil solve the  loading issue but takes a mountain of credits if you have too many fleets.

3) if your send tx are failing with the custom RPC you can clear the rpc from settings and attempt to use ironforge again.  Do not refresh and this is after all fleets have loaded.

4) if it's taking a long time for the send tx to load in your wallet  your local setup might be wrecked, swap to a different browser or wallet.

content: If blockheight errors are your issue, seems like you'd benefit from durable nonces. I was thinking about adding those to SLYA back in congestion days but then it all got "fixed" so seemed like a moot point. The downside is the extra SOL because you need to manage and maintain a nonce account per fleet but since fleets do one thing at a time it'd work perfectly.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: the blockheight errors is just a UI issue/rpc error. there's no reason for it to be happening right now.

reply.preview: the blockheight errors is just a UI issue/rpc error. there's no reason for it to be happening right now.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: It's a txn simulation or processing error. If you create a txn with a old blockheight and hash and try to process it it'll fail. If your RPC for submitting is lagging it'll also fail because it can't validate the height for the hash you used. If you used durable nonces it skips blockheight all together and as long as that RPC has the nonce account it can validate it is not being "replayed". It'll increment that nonce and forward that txn to the leader for block inclusion.

reply.preview: It's a txn simulation or processing error. If you create a txn with a old blockheight and hash and try to process it it'll fail. If your RPC for submitting is lagging it'll also fail because it can't validate the height for the hash you used. If you used durable nonces it skips blockheight all together and as long as that RPC has the nonce account it can validate it is not being "replayed". It'll increment that nonce and forward that txn to the leader for block inclusion.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Can confirm maximum wreckage.  I've swapped to the custom RPC which mostly resolved the read layer but sending TX is very bad. Block height errors are like 90% of my tx.  I haven't seen these errors for like 2+ years back when Solana was congested.  So far my general work arounds are:  1) anyone with large amounts of ruchs/airbikes need to disband them immediately as they take up most of the rpc bandwidth when loading fleets  2) using custom rpc Wil solve the  loading issue but takes a mountain of credits if you have too many fleets.  3) if your send tx are failing with the custom RPC you can clear the rpc from settings and attempt to use ironforge again.  Do not refresh and this is after all fleets have loaded.  4) if it's taking a long time for the send tx to load in your wallet  your local setup might be wrecked, swap to a different browser or wallet.

reply.preview: Can confirm maximum wreckage. I've swapped to the custom RPC which mostly resolved the read layer but sending TX is very bad. Block height errors are like 90% of my tx. I haven't seen these errors for like 2+ years back when Solana was congested. So far my general work arounds are: 1) anyone with large amounts of ruchs/airbikes need to disband them immediately as they take up most of the rpc bandwidth when loading fleets 2) using custom rpc Wil solve the loading issue but takes a mountain of credits if you have too many fleets. 3) if your send tx are failing with the custom RPC you can clear the rpc from settings and attempt to use ironforge again. Do not refresh and this is after all fleets have loaded. 4) if it's taking a long time for the send tx to load in your wallet your local setup might be wrecked, swap to a different browser or wallet.

author: Odicerous
content:
And on a slightly serious note, while I really appreciate the help people are providing with alternate RPC connections and the like, I am trying to not use workarounds wherever possible.  I do use SLYA, and I do use EveEye often, but I'm really trying to approach this game as a new(ish) user would. How finished is the experience?
 So far we have been running full speed ahead, progress, advance, iterate. And I am a big fan of where that has gotten us.  But I think the next step involves bringing in people who aren't investors, aren't play to earn crypto guys - just players.  And I think the game must be more intuitively functional for that user base.  Great strides are being made, but we have a ways to go.  I plan to use less workarounds, forego some personal gains, and see how the common idiot (namely myself) can succeed, and provide feedback to that goal.  Just my personal perspective.

I may be naive about all this stuff, but if I can understand it then anyone here can. Then anyone (and hopefully everyone) can contribute to helping MAKE that happen. So what do we mean here? How many daily "buyers" are needed (or how much buying is NEEDED) to support a strong ZINK liquidity pool and maintain price? Is that even the right question? Break it down for us simpletons.

author: mikatachan | [ROME]
content:
“How much buying is needed?” — use a slippage-based target, not a buyer-count

A practical rule of thumb:
To keep slippage ≤ 2% for a typical sell of size S (in USD), you generally need ~50× S in effective liquidity depth on that venue.,For slippage ≤ 5%, think ~20× S.,
Concrete examples (ballpark):
If you expect $100k of sells in a short window, you want ~$5M of usable depth for ~2% slippage.,If you expect $1M of sells, you want ~$50M depth for ~2% slippage.,If you expect $5M of sells, you want ~$250M depth for ~2% slippage.,
This isn’t perfect (CEX order books behave differently than AMMs), but it’s the right mental model: depth vs. expected sell bursts, not “number of buyers.”

author: Definitely_Not_JayWalk
content: “Insatiable demand for Voice Agents right now”, Partially true — market hype > actual adoption. What’s real There is strong interest in: Appointment booking Call deflection After-hours handling First-contact triage SMBs hate: Call trees Missed calls Staffing phones What’s overstated “Insatiable” is inaccurate: Many pilots fail after 1–3 months Customer frustration is common Retention is weaker than sales decks imply Adoption is sector-specific: Works best in: Clinics Salons Real estate Simple service businesses Performs poorly in: Complex support Emotional conversations Sales-heavy contexts

reply.preview: “Insatiable demand for Voice Agents right now” , Partially true — market hype > actual adoption. author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Trying to decypher the subtext here, but the reason I want to launch C4 on z.ink at inception is because launching on Solana leads to a ton of migration headache for users. The initial migration should be relatively clean, with us transferring all assets to the CSS and implementing the single step of bridging onto z.ink for a fresh start. If we do that on Solana, users start engaging in the game, moving assets around, engaging in territory control and combat.... and then we migrate to z.ink, it's a reset of the board. Which could happen in a short period after deploying to Solana. Not to mention, configuration issues that we'd have to reconcile between the two chains. So its much, much, much cleaner to go straight to z.ink.

So its much, much, much cleaner to go straight to z.ink.

author: odvb
content: Yeah, I could have stated things more clearly. I fully get why you want to launch on z.ink directly  It makes the most sense and save a lot of money (on both sides) and headache.  But recently you suggested that z.ink may be delayed and that there is a scenario where you would go live on Solana first). That surprised me, and specifically the fact that z.ink is not ready by the time C4 is ready. Up until that remark, I had always assumed it was the other way around. And that seems to indicate to me that C4 may slip if z.ink is nearly ready by the time C4 is fully done. You'll probably postpone the launch of C4 1 or 2 months if that means it can go live on z.ink directly that way.  And that could move C4 beyond the 6-month window perhaps. Though I am assuming that is not the current plan

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: Well, we have a couple of options, one of which partially informed that marketing content. We can end the O.rigin season campaign at any time. However, without mainnet, we don't have token genesis, and people are just waiting around for those events for the real conclusion of the season. Alternatively, and where I'm at right now, is that we leave it open ended, and the airdrop zXP earnings mechanics continue until genesis. In the latter scenario we have a longer ramp to drive enthusiasm into z.ink, and in turn, attract more people to Star Atlas -- to the detriment of people who simply want us to cut the airdrop campaign early for a larger share. However, having significantly more interest in z.ink, more people engaging, and a smaller overall share for everyone is going to lead to a much better outcome, from my perspective.

author: [AEP] Freeman [FRNT]
content: Perhaps it's a difference of our lived experiences, but I would say that anyone who has been around gaming, tech, crypto and any combination thereof, has learned very quickly to have a certain amount of skepticism. Even if we set aside crypto completely, legions of "traditional" companies, especially in tech, have built their brands on hype, aggressive marketing, arguably far overpromising, etc. It's just the way the world works. It's up to each of us to absorb that, and use our own rational decision-making to see what we agree with.  My philosophy is simple; every day, I ask myself if the negatives (risks) outweigh the positives (rewards) in the project. This includes everything from the current experience, how the team is responding, how they are thinking, the roadmap, the macro, etc. Every day, I'm comfortable with the money I've put into SA, even if there is a non-zero risk of it completely evaporating. Never put in more than you can afford to lose, into anything (even government bonds). But the point is, you make the decision, and you own it. It's no one else's fault or responsibility.  The reason you're getting called out is that there is a big difference between constructive criticism and a genuine desire to help the team and community build and improve, versus highly negative, repetitive noise that does nothing positive. Myself and many other OG's have absolutely had our moments at odds with Michael, the team or other community members, but we try to do that in a objective, considerate way that doesn't drag people down.  It's too easy in this world of anonymous accounts and handles to think we can act however we want, but in a project like this, your actions and how you show up do have a consequence. You may not care about that personally, but you sure as heck shouldn't be surprised when you get called out.

author: AST [STAR]
content: Lots more money.  Google search estimates say $200 Billion this year.  $80 Billion on AAA games.  Web 3 is estimated to be around $24 Billion, and most of that is simple games.  Pus web3 is vary divided, Eth has half that market share.  And they are extractors.  They put $5 in, hoping to get $6 out.  Regular gamers will put $10/$30/$50/$80 in, then more, and more, and more, often exceeding any initial investment.  With the expectation of ZERO money back out. I believe this is the fuel Star Atlas must run on. But there is a problem.  This group expects a very polished, finished product.  Simple or complex, doesn't matter, they aren't investors.  They want to pay for something that works right now.   Can we attract some to a game in development?  I think so... but we need to do a lot of cleanup first.  Fix the broken parts. Eliminate the work-arounds.  Tie loose pieces together so that things are more intuitive.  FAQs and tutorials.  Lore intro.  Easier onboarding. Hopefully Z.ink, C4 and the net Starcomm fixes much of this.   Sounds like the team is working on all of this.  Tough job, they need to divide limited resources between the next releases, and cleaning up what we have.  But its necessary. I am patient with their efforts.  Take as long as needed.  Its got to be right. By the time C4 comes out, if SA meets the minimum level of polish, I think we can draw a lot of web2 gamers.  It can change everything.

author: Odicerous
content:
And this isn't said enough, but huge props to the ATMTA team, both past and present, who've contributed to where we are today.  Its been a monumental effort, a difficult one, and still much to do.  90% of what we post here is bitching about stuff that still needs doing/fixing/improving... but it really is amazing what has been accomplished already. 
Thank you.Similarly, there are so many great community members spending huge amounts of time promoting, testing, logging, reporting and theory crafting to improve the game and make the experience better.
Thank you all.
I am very bullish for the next six months.
Hope everyone has a great weekend.

author: ZiggyMUD
content: JavaScript is single threaded (mostly) when you have to draw the UI and then forge a txn if your CPU is chewing through the render you will not chew through that txn it gets queued.

reply.preview: JavaScript is single threaded (mostly) when you have to draw the UI and then forge a txn if your CPU is chewing through the render you will not chew through that txn it gets queued.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: If you decouple or move the work out of the main thread you get better performance. Like use the main thread to render the UI and a background worker for submissions you’ll probably see better performance. SolidJS will help lower the cost of the UI due to updating via signals. Just not sure by how much honestly

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: Metaverse will have a resurgence due to AI.  We will have smart NPCs that you can converse with and soon enough digital stores manned by voice agents that manage the online stores.  Regular "citizens" will be able to build their own homes and places online since you no longer need to code...and be able to speak about what you want to build via natural language. author: ZiggyMUD
content:
The average player coming into Star Atlas probably has the wrong thinking when it comes to the earnings pie, especially because it has never been communicated. Most standard thinking would be if the daily pie is 8M ATLAS and the current players already have huge fleets, then how will I compete for a share of that daily pie. For the average game this would be correct, but in Star Atlas this is the completely wrong way of thinking. The pie in Star Atlas does not only consist of the daily ATLAS emissions but all of the P2P opportunities to earn as well.

Earning in Star Atlas is probably closer to daily pie = Daily ATLAS emissions + players * economic activity (battles in C4, trading, crafting...)

But P2E traditionally just = daily token emissions

Star Atlas markets itself as P2E but it's not, it's big and better but markets itself the same as everyone else. (Plus Star Atlas main account has only rarely marketed SAGE in its testing UI)

How can Star Atlas showcase that more players does not shrink the daily pie but grow it?

One way could be for the DAO to use its ATLAS treasury, buy up ships and crew, and employ dozens of AI agents fleets to show the earnings potential growth. Plus create a revenue stream for the DAO. I've always wanted revenue streams but it could be marketed as growth or marketing spending 

Either way, it would be great if Star Atlas could show the potential daily earnings as a metric. As the player base increased you could visually see the earnings potential number increase, as there was more economic activity you could visually see the earnings potential number increase... Think of a lottery jackpot increasing as more people buy tickets.

If you had this, you could show what effect 100 AI agent players could add to the potential daily earnings pie both before and after. You could finally show that more players and activity equals more pie for all.

This would be like Star Atlas' GDP #

I would make it very visible to new players.

author: [COEX] BulutONI
content: Replace "could" with "will"    We need to juice the economy and have a way to both help and show new players what's possible. Most of the best players keep the best strategies to themselves or their guild for good reason, but Agents could become the best way to onboard new players and show them the potential.  I might not have loads of time to play especially not being close to a computer for 90% of my day, but I would definitely build a large fleet again if I could communicate strategies with an agent through a mobile app so they could play on my behalf.

author: [COEX] BulutONI
content: Those who think otherwise fail to realize that the models they design cannot reach an audience beyond Star Atlas’s existing core player base. If Star Atlas experiences a 200–300% increase during this period, it will witness a significant shift in its player base in real time.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content:
I'm on day 8-9 of being unable to send any transactions in Starbased via the UI. 2+ weeks of total reckage since Starcomm change. 

I've tried everything I can think of.  VPN, new browsers, new wallets,  Nothing works. It takes 2-3 minutes for Starbased UI to pop up a wallet for signature at which point it fails with a blockheight error.

I can only think of two things at this point which may be affecting it.  Geolocation and/or local machine.  I've tried a VPN but it could be related to RPC distance.   Local machine maybe my laptop is too new and causing a hang up.

Next steps -
-- Testing Hyper-v of my desktop PC 
-- Renting a windows server machine in a different location.
-- Getting claude 4.6 to run tx manually. (lol rip)

Honestly just really disappointing that I'm having to flail around in the dark here when you guys should have all the tools available to troubleshoot these issues.   C4 is going to be infinitely more complex so if we can't get this right... I don't see C4 going any better.

Next steps - -- Testing Hyper-v of my desktop PC -- Renting a windows server machine in a different location. -- Getting claude 4.6 to run tx manually. (lol rip) Honestly just really disappointing that I'm having to flail around in the dark here when you guys should have all the tools available to troubleshoot these issues. C4 is going to be infinitely more complex so if we can't get this right... I don't see C4 going any better.

author: 영양주사Ustur
content: Coin price is an indicator and result of everything. The number or ratio of coin releases is not important, and the price of Star Atlas coins cannot escape from the death spiral because whales sell them without paying attention to the coin price (macro-coin market atmosphere is separate). It is best not to let whales sell coins, but the next best thing is to earn tethers rather than the Star Atlas coin you got. Then, the value of the developer's Star Atlas assets will also increase, resulting in a virtuous cycle of cooperation with other places, alliances, rising ship prices, and prices due to new investors.

I’ve just submitted a PIP, and whether you agree with it or not, I’d really appreciate it if you could share your thoughts.

TLDR: ATOM has run out of Naabathon funds and is currently operating at a loss. We need to understand whether it still makes sense to keep working on this, or if this has effectively become a one-sided effort.
This PIP will clearly play a key role in determining the future of the product, asking for funds and offering Atom Cloud for free until C4.

Thanks in advance for your time, I truly appreciate it.

⁠unknown

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content: I had a feeling this was going to happen.  Unfortunately building a business is more then just building the technology. You have to have a plan in place to get users.  And a revenue model.    I can only speak from personal experience but I was never personally approached to use ATOM.  Nor was ROME approached from what I can tell.  Also not sure how I feel about making it a "free service".  If DAO is going to fund you I'd rather they fund you and you also keep charging your customers.  I'd also like to see a much larger drive to try and get customers, even from within the current base.  I also think you have the expertise to build out new functions.  Sage UI is dead for myself and most of the large players.  I brought up a PIP earlier with Ryden to finish building the Eveeye UI with the uncompleted manual instructions. But I don't see why your team couldnt compete with it.  Overall I think your budget is super cheap and reasonable my only concern is whether you will succeed even with more users if you can't monetize the users you already have.  e.g. you didn't mention any revenue in the pip to offset the budget so im guessing its zero?  I'll likely vote yes because the budget is super reasonable for the tech but it would be nice to see some of my points above considered.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: Hey Bryan, I understand you, if ATOM were to close I think I would simply try to run it locally somehow, exactly like you - I'm not inclined to use third-party software for sensitive things, so believe me, I get it.  ATOM entered development pause months ago because we were planning to invest hours for the migration, but the time we had available ran out before reaching the goal since C4 went a bit beyond our predictions and we can't stay active until that date. The idea has always been to move away more and more from mere automation to build at a higher meta level, but in any case you need to have an automation base to leverage, which is Atom-core that will remain as a legacy to the community as an open source project but won't see the update to C4, unless Atom survives ofc.  And yes, the budget is documented and it's meant only to cover operational expenses in exchange for free usage until C4, where a decision will have to be made anyway. No ATOM dev has ever taken a dollar of profit, the Naabathon money was all spent to keep the servers running so let's say we're at peace with our decision, just sorry about the market conditions.  A small note: Atom has been break-even (in terms of budget) for some time, from what I can tell you, ATOM has been the choice of small players because keeping a PC on for automation is only justified above a certain threshold of transactions per day - below that threshold ATOM is more convenient. Example: for me to keep my PC on 24/7, I spend 30 euros per month (which are EUROS not SOL or ATLAS, so it's a fixed amount), with ATOM I spend about 0.12 SOL per month, for me a self-hosted solution only makes sense if SOL is at ATH, otherwise it's significantly worse (30 euros vs 11 dollars currently, which is 9 euros at the current exchange rate).  We all know very well that unfortunately small players are the segment of players that have been hit the hardest and that the answer to this is only Zink, but for ATOM time has run out unfortunately.  Thanks for your message though, much appreciated!

author: ROME | Earther [ROME]
content: Interesting view!  I feel just the opposite.  The UE5 updates are already looking promising and make good eye candy now.  And they will continue to improve and attract interest long term. But we have a long way to go, there are many high quality games, and we just aren't going to blow the competition out of the water with fancy graphics or our interface.    I think C4 just needs to complement the UE5 promise with a functional, living economy.  If it has fun and evolving game loops, it will work in any format.  This is groundbreaking stuff and will be its own draw for new players.  Browser based is fine for now.  Available to a much wider audience and allows for quick and nimble changes.  Devs will need to be 100% focused on C4 working smoothly and reliably.  They will be fixing issues, adjusting economic levers, and hopefully solving new, un-imagined problems caused by tons of new players.  I don't think they should even consider Fleet Command until C4 internals are functioning flawlessly.       And yes, FC will bring in a whole new level or player!  But if we attract them, we better have a LOT more fleshed out in UE5 to keep them busy and engaged.

reply.preview: Interesting view! I feel just the opposite. The UE5 updates are already looking promising and make good eye candy now. And they will continue to improve and attract interest long term. But we have a long way to go, there are many high quality games, and we just aren't going to blow the competition out of the water with fancy graphics or our interface. I think C4 just needs to complement the UE5 promise with a functional, living economy. If it has fun and evolving game loops, it will work in any format. This is groundbreaking stuff and will be its own draw for new players. Browser based is fine for now. Available to a much wider audience and allows for quick and nimble changes. Devs will need to be 100% focused on C4 working smoothly and reliably. They will be fixing issues, adjusting economic levers, and hopefully solving new, un-imagined problems caused by tons of new players. I don't think they should even consider Fleet Command until C4 internals are functioning flawlessly. And yes, FC will bring in a whole new level or player! But if we attract them, we better have a LOT more fleshed out in UE5 to keep them busy and engaged.

author: Odicerous
content: We all do it. I am certainly guilty of it... rather often, tbh. >  > The mental phenomenon of overlooking one's own flaws due to a heavy focus on progress is rooted in cognitive biases, particularly confirmation bias and cognitive dissonance. By overvaluing growth, the mind protects self-worth, a defensive mechanism that avoids acknowledging shortcomings. This, paired with a cognitive fixation on achievements, blinds individuals to areas still needing improvement.

A brainstorming session with Vantarah would be perfect but we are not allowed to see too much beyond the ATMTA's curtain.

What we need here, though, is more devs.  I don't give suggestions in SACK anymore because I know that everything I'm testing has already been scheduled and worked on a couple of months ago, at a bare minimum. I can start doing it again but truth is, I don't know what's being developed right now and there's the risk I just create noise or waste devs' time. A brainstorming session with Vantarah would be perfect but we are not allowed to see too much beyond the ATMTA's curtain.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: I love the efficiency AI is beginning to bring, but I also fear it might devalue human effort to an irreparable degree—concentrating rewards on ownership of machines while turning most people’s labor into a line item.   And when it comes to gaming, I fear it will flood the space with synthetic content so cheap and infinite that human-made worlds, mods, art, and stories will struggle to find an audience - let alone be valued.  As in The Matrix, humanity may go down fighting their own creation until resistance becomes futile, and "choice" is replaced by assimilation or worst.

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: I believe that the way we define "labor" needs to adapt to these quickly-changing technologies. In fact, the definition of gaming, leisure, relationships, work, and philosophy are all going to rapidly shift over the coming years.  Although our brains are not necessarily equipped to change so suddenly, our ability to be an early adopter of AI could create a significant shift in our culture between the producers and the consumers — even more than what exists already.  This is happening. So we each must decide to fight against the current or ride the wave as best we can.

content: the 5-10% refers to the fact that some persistence in price differencials seems to exist over time, ie you can indeed make some money by following the trends

author: [-U-] OrionorbitMUD
content: and this has been measured and verified in pretty much every stock market that exists, i really dont get why people still fight over this

author: [-U-] OrionorbitMUD
content: and to that let me add the little anecdote that after decades of experience in european financial services i have never served any hedge fund that tries to make money predicting patterns. I have served hedge funds doing any kind of strategy imaginable, from predicting economic data from satelite imagery to statistical arbitrage and high frequency trading, not once have i met someone who ever made money on the markets using paterns and technical analysis. author: [HEIM] CapThor
content: I have been involved with AI for a bit now (even running a Voice AI company) and never really felt that OMG moment until OpenClaw came out.  I was hesitant for a while since there are just so many things to keep track of in this space but I jumped in this week after reading that post on Twitter (and seeing what you are building) and now having set up my OpenClaw agent I have a sense that the world has just changed forever.    Once these things become more available to everyday users it will fundamentally change the world.  Like the article says most people are using free plans and have no idea of the tech that we currently have.  I'm still trying to grasp the full realization of it myself.  The world is about to have a major change sooner than anybody thinks  and I don't think anybody is ready for it.  I don't see how we avoid mass layoffs within the next year given what we have now.  Now I just need to find a way to control my token usage/cost.

author: [AEP] Mendizo
content:
What's interesting to me about this topic above (e.g. the "Unseen") is that exact same thing applies to the likes of SA. Everyone of course has a great imagination to put in this much capital into a brand new venture. But it's the "Unseen" even in our own project here that excites me most. There are game loops, ways to play, ways to enjoy that not even Michael (who we can safely say has dreamed the most of possibilities out of anyone) can see yet. 

Just like the article has stated where having these LLMs and agentic technologies start by solving the "how to code" problem is critical, I see the last few years of SA in the same way. The significant amounts of iterating, testing, taking a step back where needed, the strategic decision to do this on-chain, open API and interface creation by 3rd parties, etc., has all (and continues to be) in some ways that first step. As we move forward at pace, I don't think anyone here can even begin to guess what kinds of experiences we'll have in SA in 5 years' time. I'm here for the Unseen.

As Star Atlas evolves toward deeper on-chain identity and ownership, what role could something like an Unstoppable domain (e.g. bc.unstoppable) realistically play inside the ecosystem long term?

Identity layer? Payment routing? Reputation anchor? Or mostly symbolic at this stage?

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: What's interesting to me about this topic above (e.g. the "Unseen") is that exact same thing applies to the likes of SA. Everyone of course has a great imagination to put in this much capital into a brand new venture. But it's the "Unseen" even in our own project here that excites me most. There are game loops, ways to play, ways to enjoy that not even Michael (who we can safely say has dreamed the most of possibilities out of anyone) can see yet.   Just like the article has stated where having these LLMs and agentic technologies start by solving the "how to code" problem is critical, I see the last few years of SA in the same way. The significant amounts of iterating, testing, taking a step back where needed, the strategic decision to do this on-chain, open API and interface creation by 3rd parties, etc., has all (and continues to be) in some ways that first step. As we move forward at pace, I don't think anyone here can even begin to guess what kinds of experiences we'll have in SA in 5 years' time. I'm here for the Unseen.

author: Chet Roberts [ROME]
content: I'm not familiar with those namespaces, but we don't really intend to support them on z.ink. I suppose it's something we eventually could. Our immediate priority, however, would be introducing the .ink namespace. The primary benefit to those is human readability. Instead of copying/pasting a pubkey, double checking the characters, and still shitting yourself that you might have pasted it wrong, you can paste or type the name.ink, and have greater confidence you're sending it to the right person (or receiving)

author: Rick Moonkin [STAR]
content:
My AI assistant Nyx: 

Briefing Report: Integrating Advanced Artificial Intelligence for Star Atlas (February 2026)

Star Atlas stands at a turning point.Not merely “add AI”—but become the first metaverse where AI is the nervous system.In February 2026 the technology already exists to replace hundreds of humans with a dozen autonomous agents that work faster, more accurately, and without fatigue.

---

Intelligence: The 2026 AI Landscape

The age of simple chatbots is over.We are in the era of agent teams that decide for themselves who does what and execute tasks from architecture to deployment.

Claude 4.6 (Opus) — philosopher and architect. One-million-token context, 94.5% logical consistency. Perfect for high-level design: ensures a change in XP accrual doesn’t break the entire economy. Can hold the whole repository in memory and orchestrate internal agent teams.,
GPT-5.3-Codex — aggressive engineer. 77.3% on Terminal-Bench. Writes code three times faster than Claude. Self-heals UE5 builds, optimizes RPC, generates Zinc binaries more efficiently than standard compilers. The default choice for DevOps and rapid prototyping.,
Gemini 3 Pro — multimodal designer. Understands video, space, and “vibe.” Can watch Holosim gameplay, spot visual anomalies. Excels at generating adaptive SAGE Labs UI from plain text prompts.,
Grok 4.1 — real-time analyst and mathematical genius. Live access to X data, 93.3% on AIME. Best at monitoring community sentiment, correlating in-game events with ATLAS price, and calculating optimal emission paths in Big Brain mode. Critical for OMO and dynamic fiscal policy.,

author: Rick Moonkin [STAR]
content:
---

Key Application Vectors

Zinc & BlockchainThe move to Zinc is the correct response to 30% player losses on fees. But Rust + SVM demands surgical precision.AI agents (Rig, Solana Agent Kit) write and deploy code autonomously. Claude verifies zProfile cryptography. Codex minimizes compute units. Sherlock AI and Savant Chat hunt vulnerabilities deeper than any human audit.,
UE5 & Content3000 planets cannot be built by hand.PCG + Google Genie 3 + Nvidia Meshtron = worlds generated on the fly that react to player actions.Ludus AI writes C++ and Blueprints inside the editor. Codex refactors tangled include chains. Gemini perfects visuals for tech artists.,
NPC & “Soul”   AIRIS from SingularityNET + Claude-driven dialogue = NPCs that remember you, evolve relationships, and generate quests from global events.Not scripted. Real memory and evolution.,
ATMTA OrganizationShift from hierarchy to agent orchestration.Programmers become validators and AI curators.Designers become coaches of generation algorithms.,
---

author: Rick Moonkin [STAR]
content:
Risks

AI Slop — accumulation of garbage code that becomes unmaintainable.,Dependency on proprietary APIs — risk of infrastructure capture.,Mitigation: develop sovereign models on open weights (Llama 4, Mistral) fine-tuned on Galia Expanse data.,
---

Recommendation: Course of Action

Deploy a hybrid stack:Claude 4.6 for architecture and agent teams.GPT-5.3-Codex for DevOps and code.Gemini 3 Pro for visuals and UI.Grok 4.1 for OMO and sentiment analysis.,
Before C4 PTR, launch a fleet of thousands of AI agents for economy stress-testing and exploit hunting.,
Begin experiments with Genie 3 for living, reactive planets.,
Retrain the team: every member becomes an AI curator, not a routine executor.,
Lay the foundation for a sovereign model on open weights—so no one can pull the plug.,
---

author: [Λ] Sawyn [BR] [STAR]
content:
Hello, I'm coming here with an open mind to exchange ideas that could encourage players to stay in the game.

Considering all web3 games based on token pools, where issuance is always bigger than consumption, we always see the games go to zero and die because the biggest incentive for investors is effectively to squeeze as much as possible out.

I believe that SA is going in the same direction and some simple measures can change this course.

4 - Focus on fewer developments. Currently, we have many game modes, but none are well-developed. Select 2 or at most 3 to continue with; of these, I would suggest:
Based/C4: focusing primarily on resource generation, mission systems, and atlas issuance.,Shooter: a competitive mode, focused on attracting serious players, those who won't mind spending a lot of money on a skin.,Galia: create one star system at a time well-made (in the same vein as Star Citizen).,
Forget racing, forget Siege, forget fleet command... you end up wasting time and money developing and improving many things, and none of them become something launchable and attractive to real players. Compare this to standard game releases; they don't risk having 300 gameplay modes, they focus on one, do it well, and then move on to the next.

5 - Manage the issuance of Atlas tokens through missions and market, a mission system where the player will effectively have to act to receive something. There are several ways to incorporate this into the Based system: combat missions, scanning missions, basic resource delivery missions. This makes it possible to make the issuance more stable since you can give more or less Atlas tokens in missions depending on the number of active players. NPCs can dynamically buy resources, where each star base has a resource need, and this need will be met by the players.

author: [Λ] Sawyn [BR] [STAR]
content: Example: base X needs Iron, so the value of iron bought at that base will be higher than normal in the market, thus there will be a greater flow of players wanting to take iron to that base to make the sale. But as players sell, the base stabilizes and consequently approaches the game's standard value. If players continue to sell there, the base will become saturated with iron, and its value will begin to decline, eventually becoming unviable for the player to deposit resources in that base.

author: [Λ] Sawyn [BR] [STAR]
content:
As you can see, I started with number 4, as numbers 1, 2, and 3 might scare current investors quite a bit... however, if you want me to put the idea here, I'd be happy to do so.

There's a popular saying that goes like this: "Many arrows don't guarantee hitting the target, and many targets confuse the archer."

We have many targets in SA, and they are all really far away.

Sorry for anything, I believe in the project and see a lot of potential, and that's why I care.

@SW4GN3R

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: You're absolutely right - we're on the outside and don't see exactly how the SA team is structured and working right now.  That's precisely why I'm writing this: looking at how dramatically AI has transformed coding, game development, design, narrative, procedural generation, and even internal studio workflows over the last 12-18 months - this isn't "the future" anymore, it's here and accelerating exponentially.  I genuinely hope the Star Atlas team is keeping a close eye on these shifts and already integrating them at a level that will let the project not just survive, but actually leap ahead. Because right now, this feels like one of the strongest levers to catch the wave and pull away from the pack.  If internally they're still sticking to a classic, conservative approach without seriously leveraging agentic AI, subagents, LLM-driven systems, etc. - then honestly, that's really sad. Especially for those of us who truly care about SA and want to see it thrive and grow.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: Year ago I would have 100% agreed with you.   That was exactly how frontier models behaved in 2023- early 2024: great at remixing existing patterns, painful and hallucination-heavy on truly novel stuff without heavy hand-holding.  But the delta between then and now is massive - and pretty much every top-tier engineer / research lead I follow openly says the same thing.  Quick TL;DR :   Dario Amodei (CEO of Anthropic) at Dreamforce literally said that Claude is already writing ~90% of the code inside Anthropic itself. Yes - one of the leading models is autonomously generating the majority of production code for its own creators, under senior oversight of course, but still 90%.  We're past the "AI can only make a quick landing page" phase.   It's now writing real internal tooling, infrastructure, features - and doing it faster than humans ever could alone. The people pushing the bleeding edge aren't puppeteering line-by-line anymore; they're reviewing, steering high-level architecture, and letting the model fill in 5-10x more than before.  Of course it's not magic and still needs strong engineers - but the leverage is already ridiculous, and it's only getting steeper.  So when I talk about AI being a necessity for something like Star Atlas to survive and pull ahead - I mean exactly this level of integration, not just "throwing ChatGPT at Unity scripts".

author: [Λ] Sawyn [BR] [STAR]
content:
One thing I see as wrong about actions taken regarding the value of Atlas is that people want the company to be held responsible for its value by burning through company capital.

We already have little real capital entering the project's development; removing more capital is condemning the company to abandon the project altogether.

This is due to the Web 3 coin pool system, which depends on the inflow and outflow of money for the currency to gain or lose value.

What should be done is an incentive for the money to remain in the game.

I'll explain a proposal shortly.

author: [Λ] Sawyn [BR] [STAR]
content:
Atlas should be an in-game item. A direct, consumable item with the possibility of profit, not an item that is always targeted for extraction.

That said, the idea is based on the principle of priority. It defines that the currency has its greatest value as usable. That said, within the game it maintains its value.

Example: if I need to buy 1 million iron for 100 Atlas and I have 100 Atlas in my wallet, I can buy it normally. If I don't have it, I can exchange it at Jupiter and deposit 100 Atlas into the game and buy it normally.

However, what changes is that withdrawing the 100 Atlas from my game profile to my external personal wallet will incur a 50% fee. In other words, if I decide to remove this value from the game to exchange it at Jupiter, I lose half. Of this percentage, 50% would be sent to the DAO and the other 50% would return to the wallet that makes payments executed by the team within the game.

In other words, this mechanic will prioritize keeping the Atlases in the game without removing the possibility of the player receiving something for playing.

That said, some things need to be changed:

(continues)

author: [Λ] Sawyn [BR] [STAR]
content:
The player's profile will have an "Atlas" stored. It has a 1:1 equivalence with the Atlas on the market. (Everything can be linked to Atlas Prime),
Any Atlas issued by the team must go to the player's profile and not to the external wallet.,
Other systems should also interact with this wallet in the deposit : ship rentals, stake rewards, LP, etc.,
It may be possible to reduce the fee from 50% to as low as 20% using the Atlas staking system, but when the stake is removed, it must go to the game profile. This would prevent exploits of pooling all Atlases into one account.,
Adjust Atlas issuance according to the items mentioned in the previous text.,

author: [Λ] Sawyn [BR] [STAR]
content:
Obviously, anyone who's only here to extract value from the Atlas pool, until it reaches a point where it's no longer playable, will be against the idea.

After all, when this project dies, these people will move on to the next one.

But anyone here who knows any game that's over 20 years old knows that the money in the game is prioritized to keep the game going.

This mechanic doesn't make it impossible for the player to earn money; it just prioritizes reinvestment and encourages players to stay in the game.

​Even in the real world, there are penalties for manipulating market dynamics. Why shouldn't that be the case here? They must transform the market into an environment where everyone can trade fairly.

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: You're absolutely right - we're on the outside and don't see exactly how the SA team is structured and working right now.  That's precisely why I'm writing this: looking at how dramatically AI has transformed coding, game development, design, narrative, procedural generation, and even internal studio workflows over the last 12-18 months - this isn't "the future" anymore, it's here and accelerating exponentially.  I genuinely hope the Star Atlas team is keeping a close eye on these shifts and already integrating them at a level that will let the project not just survive, but actually leap ahead. Because right now, this feels like one of the strongest levers to catch the wave and pull away from the pack.  If internally they're still sticking to a classic, conservative approach without seriously leveraging agentic AI, subagents, LLM-driven systems, etc. - then honestly, that's really sad. Especially for those of us who truly care about SA and want to see it thrive and grow.

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: I offered this alternative to what was implemented recently for OMO buybacks. Would be interested to hear your thoughts about it.  ATLAS Marketplace Buyback Program,The buyback program is a deterministic lottery that incentivizes competitive ATLAS sell-order pricing on the Star Atlas marketplace. It randomly selects and fills eligible ATLAS sell orders from the operator's connected wallet each epoch — and because the algorithm is seeded by an on-chain slot hash, anyone can independently verify the results.  How It Works,Snapshot, — A marketplace snapshot is taken at a specific Solana slot, capturing all sell orders and the Jupiter ATLAS/USDC price. The slot hash (32 bytes) becomes the deterministic seed.  Oracle Pricing, — For each asset, a fair price is calculated: Gather all USDC sell orders for the asset,If the asset has fewer than 10 USDC sell orders (the order threshold), it is excluded entirely — there isn't enough market depth to establish a reliable price. This threshold can be adjusted as necessary,Otherwise, compute the 25th percentile (P25) of USDC sell prices and convert it to ATLAS using the Jupiter ATLAS price, Eligibility Filtering, — ATLAS sell orders are checked against a spread allowance of 500 basis points (5%): maxEligiblePrice = oraclePrice × 1.05,Orders priced at or below this cap are eligible; orders above it are excluded,This ensures only competitively priced orders enter the lottery,Can be adjusted higher or lower as necessary, Deterministic Lottery, — Using the slot hash as a seed: A ChaCha20 PRNG (RFC 8439) generates the random stream,Assets are canonically sorted (by mint, byte-wise) then Fisher-Yates shuffled,Each asset's orders are canonically sorted (by pubkey) then shuffled,A round-robin fill walks through shuffled assets one at a time, filling one order per asset per round, until the budget is exhausted. Will result in partial fills, Execution, — Winning fills are validated on-chain (orders still exist and aren't already filled), then executed as marketplace transactions signed by the operator's wallet.  Key Parameters,Order Threshold — 10 — Minimum USDC sell orders required for an asset to be eligible Spread Allowance — 500 bps (5%) — Maximum price above P25 oracle for an ATLAS order to qualify  Verification,Results can be independently verified: import a result file and the system re-runs the lottery with the same snapshot, seed, and config. If fill count, total spent, and fill order all match, the result is confirmed deterministic.

It randomly selects and fills eligible ATLAS sell orders from the operator's connected wallet each epoch — and because the algorithm is seeded by an on-chain slot hash, anyone can independently verify the results. How It Works , Snapshot , — A marketplace snapshot is taken at a specific Solana slot, capturing all sell orders and the Jupiter ATLAS/USDC price. The slot hash (32 bytes) becomes the deterministic seed. Oracle Pricing , — For each asset, a fair price is calculated: Gather all USDC sell orders for the asset , If the asset has fewer than 10 USDC sell orders (the order threshold), it is excluded entirely — there isn't enough market depth to establish a reliable price. This threshold can be adjusted as necessary , Otherwise, compute the 25th percentile (P25) of USDC sell prices and convert it to ATLAS using the Jupiter ATLAS price , Eligibility Filtering , — ATLAS sell orders are checked against a spread allowance of 500 basis points (5%): maxEligiblePrice = oraclePrice × 1.05 , Orders priced at or below this cap are eligible; orders above it are excluded , This ensures only competitively priced orders enter the lottery , Can be adjusted higher or lower as necessary , Deterministic Lottery , — Using the slot hash as a seed: A ChaCha20 PRNG (RFC 8439) generates the random stream , Assets are canonically sorted (by mint, byte-wise) then Fisher-Yates shuffled , Each asset's orders are canonically sorted (by pubkey) then shuffled , A round-robin fill walks through shuffled assets one at a time, filling one order per asset per round, until the budget is exhausted. Will result in partial fills , Execution , — Winning fills are validated on-chain (orders still exist and aren't already filled), then executed as marketplace transactions signed by the operator's wallet. Key Parameters , Order Threshold — 10 — Minimum USDC sell orders required for an asset to be eligible Spread Allowance — 500 bps (5%) — Maximum price above P25 oracle for an ATLAS order to qualify Verification , Results can be independently verified: import a result file and the system re-runs the lottery with the same snapshot, seed, and config. author: [Λ] Sawyn [BR] [STAR]
content:
The biggest problem is knowing if the team will want to make a change in this direction, to reduce/discourage the removal of Atlas from the game.

Another thing I think is very important is:

Drastically reduce the development of C4 after launch, leaving more of the adjustments to the team.

Focus 100% on making the shooter mode competitive and functional, reducing glitches, creating a ranking system, etc.

Forget about other game modes for a while, focus on this one, finish it, and get players to come and interact and add to the ecosystem. We have so many games that only have this (CS, Overwatch, Valorant...). After this mode has 3D characters, a battle pass system, and loot boxes for rare skins, when this mode is launchable, return to focusing on and expanding C4.

Add unique missions, lore, functionalities, local merchants, etc. Focus on making it as complete as possible. Meanwhile, the Unreal team is refining Fleet Command...

Forget about the other game modes for now; we have so many unfinished game modes that it's frustrating to try and introduce SA to an outsider.

author: [Λ] Sawyn [BR] [STAR]
content:
I'm not saying you can't develop race mode, siege mode, etc.

But if you have one ready-made, functional, attractive mode, something to be the "flagship" and keep players active while other things are being developed... you bring back the shine.

Following this reasoning, after finishing the shooting mode, create a fleet command mode already linked to C4, then move on to Galia mode, because the universe will already exist; it will only be a matter of inserting points of interest on the planets, in space as a whole.,

At the moment we have several targets, all wanting to be ready at the same time.

author: [Λ] Sawyn [BR] [STAR]
content:
I was discussing this today in the guild group, considering a scenario where everything I mentioned above is feasible (and it is feasible).

Besides limiting the issuance of atlases to the market, focusing strictly on prioritization for the player, it creates a logical and objective line about the game's stages, from Free to Play to Play to Earn.

Imagine a new player, they arrive in the game and have 4 free characters available to master up to level 20. When they reach level 20 on all 4 characters, they receive their first mintable crew pack. At the same time, they generated a lot of Ink and atlases to sell. They won't be able to withdraw them from the game due to the stake requirement, but they will be able to minte the pack. Now this player has 5 characters they can use in Based (C4 or whatever it's called). They can use the atlases to buy basic resources, start crafting, and gather resources by playing... this is a logical sequence and rewards those who actually play.

If the player wants, they can put in money and speed up the process, buy ships, buy atlases, increase their stake to start getting some return from the game... but notice that this is a big difference from other games where someone comes in, creates 30 free accounts, farms the currency, sells it on the market, and ends up destroying the entire game ecosystem out of pure greed.

content:
Boris now says AI writes 100% of his code. He barely touches the keyboard for actual coding anymore.
Andrej Karpathy (ex-Tesla AI lead, OpenAI co-founder) also flipped from 80% manual to 80% AI in just a couple weeks at the end of 2025.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: CLAUDE.md isn't the memory. It is used for a general project description (for humans and bots). The memory is saved outside of the project folder, e.g. in ~/.claude/projects/-home-xxx-Claude-yyy/memory First thing I did was instructing Claude to create a local Git, so I can go back if something goes wrong and also it acts as an extended memory, e.g. Claude can check why something went wrong, because it still has access to all the previous versions. I had some super complex tasks and Claude got them all right. Very impressive. But it feels very weird to speak to program.

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content:
Anyway, there’s a real hunger for Galia out there. I’ve never tried to optimize any title, thumbnail, or description, and despite that, the single-player campaign gameplay video completely overshadows the others in terms of views, likes, and watch time.

The funny thing? It’s a gameplay video with no commentary. I didn’t want to “taint” the video with a very non-english accent, so I chose to publish pure gameplay and people are still watching it.

I’m broadly aware of the team’s situation and bandwidth, so this isn’t meant to be taken as an additional development request. It’s just a (very limited) piece of information sharing about what seemed to me like the audience’s interest, based on the metrics of the three YouTube videos I’ve made over time, just to spark a bit of conversation

author: Rick Moonkin [STAR]
content:
I also watched the latest episode of Atlas Brew (192)

The team makes extensive use of AI - running a whole swarm of AI agents (Cursor + Cloud Code + Augment) to build systems and debug stuff, used AI to nail the root cause of those random Zinc crashes, and they’re baking proper safeguards straight into the game so NPCs and quests don’t hallucinate.

For me it’s now crystal clear that the SA team is fully riding the wave -they’re squeezing every last drop out of AI for development, bug fixing, and even weaving it right into the game itself. AI and blockchain are the two sharpest game-changer technologies of our time, and Star Atlas is bringing them together. That’s a killer strategic and competitive advantage.

author: Rick Moonkin [STAR]
content:
Yeah, $300/day per agent and $100k/year sounds straight-up insane at first. If you just throw raw powerful models at everything with no system, the token bill explodes fast.

But here’s the flip side:
Those crazy numbers almost always come from unoptimized runs - huge contexts on every call, using Opus for trivial stuff, no caching or dynamic context. Plenty of teams are already slashing costs 70-80% just by getting smarter (mixing cheap/fast models for routine tasks, planning first, proper caching).

That’s exactly why the swarm + “teach the model from your own mistakes” workflow we talked about is so powerful. It’s not only faster -  it’s dramatically cheaper and cleaner too.

So yeah, the concern is valid… if you do it wrong.
Do it right and a small team of sharp pros can now do what used to take entire large studios - the kind of work that once needed dozens of people, endless meetings, and massive budgets.

author: [SLY] Groove [STAR]
content:
On one side, if you have some reference point (say, local markets) that you can use to drive decisions, you can accomplish things like dynamic quests relatively trivially without AI.

Now, if you want the ability to give thousands of NPCs unique personalities - I can see where AI could help get you there. I still thing there's multiple paths here, though. One path is to leverage AI to generate the "personality code" that gets implemented into your game. This seems like it'd be a heck of a lot cheaper than having AI agents actively controlling characters.

author: ROME | Earther [ROME]
content: Still XYZ. The system generates a 3d grid inside a given volume, and anywhere the grid intersects the ground, it makes a possible navigation point there, and then gets the Up vector from the player's location to the planet center. This system also allows us to have creatures that can crawl on walls and ceilings if we wish

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: FICs being uncapped is the differences between it and LP. LP leads to pool game theory and can force big players to squeeze out little players. If the big players are focused on the more lucrative FICs they won't partipicate in the LP and thus little players can get something even though that pie is much smaller than the FIC pie.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: FICs being buy orders and can be cleaned out quickly post fill is an issue as well. If you can coordinate a wipe, the other players won't have anything to exchange.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: Personally, I don't think the answer is to remove one or the other, while keeping the survivor. I'm not a huge fan of either. But I am a huge fan of trimming any and all emissions down to one source whatever that maybe. (Also ensuring it follows the whitepaper emissions or the schedule token release plan where ever it may live.. is also ideal)

author: [Λ] Neo_AArmstrong [EC]Econ Vantarah
content: That is the intended loop. You're either engaged in combat earning emissions directly (similar to LP system), or you're mining/claim staking/crafting and selling goods and services to other players. Theoretically, you're building the components that people engaged in combat are using and losing during combat. Kind of trickle down. Combat players earn emissions but lose items/time/ATLAS via repair and respawn. And they're spending earnings with players doing the majority of manufacturing.

author: ROME | Earther [ROME]
content: Yes, I remember the discussion — when capital ships were at $6k.  But since I’m now buying capital ships at $1–1.5k each (and I think I’m among the few actually buying), I wonder whether the time has come to clarify a few points.  I’m here. And I’ll stay here. I don’t care whether capital ships are at $100 or $1,000 — compared to $30k, the difference between $500 and $1,500 doesn’t really change much.  So I ask myself: Do we really have to read PIPs from OG community members asking for a $5k salary? Are there no other solutions?  And so I’m expressing my thoughts — the thoughts of many Italians, used to living on 2k a month.  I’m not even going to transcribe the uproar that came from the reduced $5k…

author: ⪛⦿⫺ZeSKK [STAR]
content: Since we don't have our UE5 game yet, we're currently left with only Holosim, which is a browser-based RTS. I’m honestly conflicted about whether to be satisfied with this, as it’s clearly not a genre that appeals to me or, from what I can see, to a significant portion of the player base.  I know some might take offense when I say we don't have a UE5 game yet, and for that, please bear with me. However, I find it difficult to even categorize this as a 'game' due to the complete lack of content. It’s hard to label something a game when there is simply no substance to it.

author: [COEX] 𝙈𝙪𝙖𝙙'𝙙𝙞𝙗
content:
I have over 1,500 hours in Dune: Awakening, and even though it features a vast amount of repeatable content and numerous variations, people on Reddit still criticize it heavily for a lack of content and variety. This shows how crucial it is to truly understand the player base's expectations.

For us, UE5 is something we have been dreaming of for exactly six years. Naturally, when you’ve been waiting for that level of quality for so long, anything less simply fails to feel satisfying.

author: [AEP] Funcracker [AEP]
content:
I wonder if we are still planning to rely on MetaGravity for the MMO portion 

The team spent a great deal of effort on that, only to then freeze it and focus on EOS as that was the easiest way to support smooth, real-time combat.

Is the plan still to get back to this in the future? So we still have that option? 

I kinda feel like it still has the best papers for allowing us to create an MMO with very large shards (if not a single shard).

But I have not the faintest clue where this relationship stands atm.

If you have a moment @bunthius, I would love to hear your thoughts on this.

And I understand btw that this is not something that will be worked on this year.
